### Source A 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2fbbec29-f7c8-45dd-a5e1-45fe9877397f)

### Source B
It is a paradox to brand a technological innovation as tainted goods by its very name. ‘Deepfake’ is a victim of its own capabilities. Negative connotations and recent incidents have pigeonholed this innovation in the taboo zone. The rise of deepfake technology has ushered in very interesting possibilities and challenges. This synthetic media, created through sophisticated artificial intelligence algorithms, has begun to infiltrate various sectors, raising intriguing questions about its potential impact on education and employability.

The dawn of deepfake technology introduces a realm of possibilities in education. Imagine medical students engaging in lifelike surgical simulations or language learners participating in authentic conversations. The potential for deepfake to revolutionise training scenarios is vast and could significantly enhance the educational experience. Beyond simulations, deepfake can transport students to historical events through realistic reenactments or facilitate virtual field trips, transcending the boundaries of traditional education. The immersive nature of deepfake content holds the promise of making learning more engaging and memorable.




### Source C 
However, while these potential abuses of the technology are real and concerning that doesn't mean we should turn a blind eye to the technology’s potential when using it responsibly, says Jaime Donally, a well-known immersive learning expert. 

“Typically, when we're hearing about it, it's in terms of the negative – impersonation and giving false claims,” Donally says. “But really, the technology has the power of bringing people from history alive through old images that we have using AI.” 

Donally, a former math teacher and instructional technologist, has written about how a type of deep fake technology called deep nostalgia technology that went viral in 2021 can allow students to form a stronger connection with the past and their personal family heritage. The technology, available on the MyHeritage app, allows images to uploaded that are then turned into short animations thanks to AI technology. 

Here are some of the ways in which teachers can utilize deep fake technology in the classroom utilizing the MyHeritage app.
Teachers have used the deep fake technology in the My Heritage app to bring historical figures such as Amelia Earhart and Albert Einstein to life. One teacher Donally has communicated with used an animation of Frederick Douglass (above) to help students connect with Douglass’ famous 1852 speech about the meaning of the Fourth of July to Black enslaved Americans. Another teacher has plans to use the app to have students interview a historical figure and create dialogue for them, then match the dialogue to the animation.

Donally herself has paired animations she's created with other types of immersive technology. “I layered it on top in augmented reality,” she says. “When you scan the photo of my grandfather, it all came to life. And it became something that was much more relevant to see in your real-world space.” 

With proper supervision, students can use the technology to animate images of family members or local historical figures, and can experiment with augmented reality (AR) in the process. 
“It makes you want to learn more,” Donally says of animations created using deep fake technology. “It drives you into kind of the history and understanding a bit more, and I think it also helps you identify who you are in that process.” 

### Source D 
Education platforms are harnessing deepfake technology to create AI tutors that provide customised support to students. Rather than a generic video lecture, each learner can get tailored instruction and feedback from a virtual tutor who speaks their language and adjusts to their level.

For example, Anthropic built Claude, an AI assistant designed specifically for education. Claude can answer students’ natural language questions, explain concepts clearly, and identify knowledge gaps.

Such AI tutors make learning more effective, accessible, and inclusive. Students feel like they have an expert guide helping them master new skills and material.

AI and deepfake technology have enormous potential to enhance workforce training and education in immersive new ways too.
As Drew Rose, CSO and founder of cybersecurity firm Living Security, explains, “educators can leverage deepfakes to create immersive learning experiences. For instance, a history lesson might feature a ‘guest appearance’ by a historical figure, or a science lesson might have a renowned scientist explaining complex concepts.”
Ivana Bartoletti, privacy and data protection expert at Wipro and author of An Artificial Revolution – On Power, Politics and AI envisions similar applications.

“Deepfake technologies could provide an easier and less expensive way to train and visualise,” she says.
“Students of medicine and nursing currently train with animatronic robots. They are expensive and require special control rooms. Generative AI and augmented or virtual reality headsets or practice rooms will be cheaper and allow for the generalisation, if not the gamification, of simulation.”

Medical students could gain experience diagnosing and treating simulated patients, while business students could practice high-stakes scenarios like negotiations without real-world consequences.
These immersive, gamified environments enabled by AI and deepfakes also have vast potential for corporate training.

Bartoletti notes, “A similar use case could be made for other types of learning that require risky and skill-based experiences. The Air Force uses AI as adversaries in flight simulators, and humans have not beaten the best AIs since 2015.

parts.content: With reference to the sources and your own knowledge, discuss whether the use of deepfakes in the educational sector is an incremental or transformational change.
parts.markscheme:
### Possible Responses

- Definition of deepfakes and how they work $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Explanation of incremental vs transformational change $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

#### Argument for Incremental Change

- Deepfakes are an extension/evolution of existing technologies like photoshop, video editing etc. $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Simply enhancing/automating existing capabilities rather than introducing entirely new ones $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Examples of how deepfakes could be used in an incremental way in education (e.g. virtual guest lectures, language learning) $\blacksquare$ <marks>2 marks</marks>

#### Argument for Transformational Change 

- Deepfakes represent a paradigm shift in how audio/video content can be created and manipulated $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Potential to completely transform learning experiences and educational models $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Examples of transformational applications (e.g. personalized learning avatars, virtual reconstructions of historical events) $\blacksquare$ <marks>2 marks</marks>

#### Other Relevant Points

- Ethical concerns around deepfakes (e.g. consent, privacy, misinformation) $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Regulatory challenges and need for guidelines $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>
A well-balanced response considering both perspectives is expected. Credit should be given for well-substantiated examples and arguments from either side.
</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the challenges of implementing algorithmic transparency and accountability in criminal justice, particularly with “black box” algorithms.
parts.markscheme:
### Key Points

- Algorithmic transparency and accountability are important for ensuring fairness and due process in criminal justice systems
- "Black box" algorithms lack transparency, making it difficult to understand how decisions are made
- This can lead to biased or discriminatory outcomes, violating principles of justice

### Possible Arguments

- Lack of transparency means defendants/public cannot scrutinize algorithms for bias or errors <marks>1</marks>
- Opaque decision-making processes undermine due process and right to fair trial <marks>1</marks>
- Algorithms may perpetuate historical biases in data against minority groups <marks>1</marks>
- Proprietary algorithms used by private companies are not open to public audit <marks>1</marks>
- Difficulty in assigning accountability when errors/biases occur in complex systems <marks>1</marks>

### Counterarguments

- Algorithms can be more objective/consistent than human decision-making <marks>1</marks>
- Some privacy/security is needed to prevent gaming of algorithms <marks>1</marks>
- Transparency could reveal sensitive information about individuals <marks>1</marks>
- Auditing/testing can validate algorithms without full transparency <marks>1</marks>

### Potential Solutions

- Regulatory oversight and auditing requirements for algorithms <marks>1</marks>
- Open-sourcing algorithms for public scrutiny and accountability <marks>1</marks>
- Human oversight/ability to override algorithmic decisions <marks>1</marks>
- Ensuring representative and unbiased training data <marks>1</marks>

<note>Credit all relevant points from both sides. Maximum 12 marks.</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the potential of immersive media, such as VR and AR, in transforming learning experiences, considering both engagement and accessibility challenges.
parts.markscheme:
### Potential benefits of immersive media (VR/AR) for learning experiences

- Increased engagement and immersion in learning materials/environments $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Ability to visualize and interact with abstract concepts in 3D space
  - Experiential/situated learning through simulated environments

- Accessibility to learning experiences otherwise difficult/impossible $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Virtual field trips to remote locations/dangerous environments
  - Exploring microscopic/astronomical scales through scale manipulation

### Challenges of immersive media for learning

- Technical limitations and costs $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Hardware requirements (headsets, sensors, computing power)
  - Software development complexity for high-fidelity experiences

- Potential adverse physiological effects $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Motion sickness, eye strain, disorientation
  - Ergonomic issues from prolonged headset use

- Accessibility challenges $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Cost barriers for hardware/software adoption
  - Difficulties in adapting content for disabilities (e.g. vision, mobility)

### Strategies to address challenges

- Iterative design and user testing $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Identify and mitigate potential usability/accessibility issues
  - Adapt experiences based on user feedback

- Blended learning approaches $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>  
  - Integrate immersive media strategically with other instructional methods
  - Avoid overreliance on immersive tech for all learning activities

<note>Maximum 8 marks. Markers should use discretion in awarding marks for valid points not listed here.</note>

questions.specification:
**Voice simulation**

Lyrebird voice-cloning software was created by three university students from Montreal. This technology was originally developed to change the lives of people who have lost their voice. These people can use the software to create a new voice by choosing one from samples provided in the software.

However, Lyrebird can also clone voices. Lyrebird claims that it can clone a person’s voice in just 60 seconds. Users only need to access the website, create an account, and generate a voice print by repeating a number of predetermined sentences. Although the cloning is not perfect, the developers of Lyrebird believe that both the time to clone the voice and the quality of the cloning will improve in the future.

The software allows the user to alter the speech and add emotion (such as, anger, elation, and surprise), increasing the ways in which it can be used.

The initial voice print for the cloning process requires the most time and a lot of processing power. However, creating speech from the voice print is a simple process, generating around 1000 sentences almost instantly. The voice print is saved as a compressed audio file.

Lyrebird has considered the ethical issues regarding biometrics\* and acknowledges that it highlights some key societal issues. Lyrebird has taken the decision to make the software available to all in an attempt to address these issues.  
  

* * *

\* biometrics: a means of personal identification through analysis of unique physical traits or behavioural characteristics (for example, through voice patterns or fingerprints)

parts.content: To what extent do the benefits of using Lyrebird’s voice-cloning software outweigh the concerns that may arise from its use?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Benefits may include:**

*   It could help people who have lost their voice.
*   It could create better voice-activated interfaces (systems).
*   Characters in video games could have individual voices or could clone a player’s voice as a playable character (expression, identity).
*   It could provide interactive training and learning.
*   It could be used in educational software to produce a more realistic experience (systems).
*   Any device that requires speech could be enhanced by more natural voices (appointment reminders, personal assistant applications, audiobooks, etc.) (systems).
*   Parents could use the software to “read” to their children (relationships).

**Concerns may include:**

*   It could be used to create fake news or weaken the public’s confidence/trust in news of all kinds.
*   An increase in robocalls with realistic voices – scammers will be able to deceive people more easily or make fake calls to the police (values).
*   It may lead to systems that use voice as a method of authentication becoming compromised, as scammers are able to quickly create a user’s voice print and access the system.

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

### Ethical Challenges of AI in Hiring and Recruitment
Many companies use AI-driven tools to screen job applicants and rank candidates. While these systems streamline hiring, they can also introduce bias, potentially disadvantaging certain demographic groups. This raises concerns about fairness, transparency, and accountability in AI-driven recruitment.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f0934c4f-bf38-4bfb-8362-78ffc780a53b)

parts.content: To what extent should companies ensure fairness and transparency in AI-driven hiring processes, considering both the advantages of efficiency and the ethical risks of bias?
parts.markscheme:
Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>):
Basic understanding of AI in hiring, with minimal discussion on fairness and transparency.  
Limited or no evaluation of the ethical risks or efficiency benefits.
Little to no use of examples to illustrate points.

Example Points:
- "AI can make hiring more efficient by quickly screening applicants."
- "However, AI systems can introduce bias, affecting fairness."

Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>):
Clear understanding of the importance of fairness and transparency in AI-driven hiring.
Some analysis of both the efficiency benefits and ethical risks, although coverage may be imbalanced.
Examples provided to support points, but may lack detail or depth.

Example Points:
- "AI can streamline recruitment, allowing companies to process large numbers of applications quickly and objectively."
- "However, the lack of transparency in how these algorithms rank candidates can lead to biased decisions. For instance, if an algorithm is trained on past hiring data that reflects historical bias, it may disadvantage minority groups."
- Example: AI screening tools that penalize certain demographic markers or educational backgrounds.

Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>):
In-depth evaluation of the necessity for fairness and transparency in AI-driven hiring, fully addressing both the efficiency advantages and ethical risks.
Thorough analysis of the complexities and challenges in ensuring bias mitigation and accountability, with clear consideration of transparency.
Well-chosen examples used to illustrate the balance between efficiency and fairness, with potential solutions or recommendations discussed.

Example Points:
- "AI-driven recruitment tools allow companies to quickly filter applicants and identify key skills, improving efficiency in high-volume hiring processes. This can reduce costs and help to minimize certain human biases, as algorithms assess based on consistent criteria."
- "However, without transparency in how these tools operate, it is difficult to ensure fairness. For instance, Amazon's hiring algorithm was found to unintentionally penalize resumes with the word 'women,' highlighting how training data can propagate biases from past human decisions."
- Challenges: Ensuring fairness requires rigorous auditing and diverse training datasets, but these measures may reduce the efficiency benefits that AI offers. Legal and ethical accountability is another challenge, as it's often unclear who is responsible for the algorithm's biases.
- Solutions: To mitigate these risks, companies could implement regular audits and ensure transparency by publicly disclosing key aspects of how their algorithms evaluate candidates. Collaboration with external ethics boards or developing explainable AI (XAI) frameworks may help balance efficiency with fairness.

questions.specification:
### Source A: The Transformation of Journalism Through Digital Media
Digital media has disrupted traditional journalism, leading to audience fragmentation and the consolidation of news organizations. While digital platforms extend news reach, they also challenge revenue models and credibility.

For example, while Twitter provides instant news updates, it has also led to challenges in verifying information, as content can be quickly shared without fact-checking.


### Source B: Media Authenticity and Deepfakes
Deepfake technology, enabled by AI, allows for realistic but fabricated videos, raising concerns about authenticity and trust. The growing prevalence of deepfakes calls for new ways to verify digital content.

For example, deepfakes can create fabricated political speeches, potentially influencing public opinion through misinformation.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/526866e2-518b-4ead-a0f5-48d92d554307)

### Source C: Ownership, Copyright, and Remix Culture in Digital Media
Digital media has encouraged a remix culture, where creators can adapt existing content. However, this has brought up issues around copyright, ownership, and the need for Creative Commons licenses to legally share work.

For instance, remixes of popular songs or videos must comply with copyright laws, unless distributed under licenses that allow adaptations.

### Source D: Media Obsolescence and Digital Preservation
Digital content can quickly become obsolete, leading to media obsolescence and necessitating digital preservation. Websites and digital files often become inaccessible without updates or archiving efforts.

For example, digital archives like the Internet Archive preserve web content, ensuring continued access to valuable digital information.

questions.specification:
### Source A: Article View on Artificial General Intelligence is overhyped
The quest for Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)—machines that can understand and learn like humans—has fueled excitement in the AI community. However, this enthusiasm may be misplaced.

Limitations of Current AI:

Despite impressive advancements in models like GPT-4, these systems lack true understanding and depend heavily on training data. They generate responses based on patterns rather than comprehension, raising doubts about their ability to replicate human-like intelligence.


Unrealistic Expectations

Tech leaders often predict AGI's arrival within a few years, overlooking the complexities of human cognition. This has led to skepticism among experts, who emphasize that AI is more likely to progress incrementally rather than suddenly achieve AGI.


A Pragmatic Approach

Focusing on narrow AI applications—designed for specific tasks—will yield more immediate benefits. Recognizing current limitations is essential for responsible AI development. In summary, while AGI is an intriguing goal, its realization is not as imminent as some claim.

### Source B: The Promise of Artificial General Intelligence
Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) represents the pinnacle of AI research, aiming to create machines that can think, learn, and adapt across various tasks like humans. Recent advancements have fueled optimism about its potential.

Technological Breakthroughs

Innovations in deep learning and neural networks have led to significant improvements in machine learning capabilities. Systems like GPT-4 demonstrate impressive problem-solving and language understanding, showcasing the progress toward AGI.

Broad Applications

AGI holds the promise of transforming industries, enhancing productivity, and solving complex global challenges. From healthcare to climate change, AGI could provide solutions that were previously unimaginable, driving innovation and efficiency.

Future Prospects

While the journey toward AGI is complex, ongoing research and development continue to push boundaries. As we build more sophisticated models, the possibility of achieving AGI becomes increasingly plausible.

parts.content: Identify two characteristics of an ‘AI winter’.
parts.markscheme:
An 'AI winter' refers to a period of reduced funding and interest in artificial intelligence (AI) research. Two characteristics of an 'AI winter' are:

- Disillusionment with AI due to overhyped promises and unrealistic expectations not being met <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Significant reduction in investment and funding for AI research projects <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the difference between artificial general intelligence (AGI) and domain-specific AI.
parts.markscheme:
Domain-specific AI refers to AI systems that are designed to perform specific tasks within a particular domain or area of expertise. These systems are trained on data and algorithms specific to that domain, and they excel at solving problems within that narrow scope. Examples include:

- Computer vision systems for object recognition or facial recognition
- Natural language processing systems for language translation or sentiment analysis
- Recommendation systems for products or content

On the other hand, artificial general intelligence (AGI) refers to the hypothetical future development of AI systems that can match or exceed human intelligence across a wide range of cognitive tasks. <marks>2 marks</marks>

AGI systems would possess general problem-solving capabilities, reasoning, and learning abilities that allow them to tackle any task that a human can perform. They would not be limited to specific domains but would have a broad, flexible intelligence that can be applied to diverse situations. <marks>2 marks</marks>

<note>No marks for examples of domain-specific AI or mentioning that AGI does not yet exist.</note>

parts.content: Use the source above, your additional source and your knowledge of the uses of AI to, compare the claims and perspectives made by the artificial intelligence researchers and the companies creating artificial intelligence solutions, and the impacts that artificial intelligence will have on society.

  - Negative impacts on employment, privacy, and social manipulation <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Call for responsible development and robust safety measures <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Possible Arguments/Perspectives from AI Companies [3 marks]

- AI companies may emphasize the benefits and economic opportunities of AI $\blacksquare$
  - Increased efficiency, productivity gains across various industries <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Potential for technological breakthroughs and scientific advancements <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
_CBR Bank_ holds a large amount of information on its customers. Some customers are concerned about the security, privacy and anonymity of their data.

For **each** of the concerns above, explain**one** policy that _CBR Bank_ could use to address the concerns of its customers.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Security:**

*   User access to data is limited to authorized personnel – to ensure data is secure during storage.
*   Username and password access is implemented – to ensure data is secure during storage.
*   A password policy is implemented (e.g., minimum length, mix of characters, changed after a given number of days etc_._).
*   Two-factor / two-step authentication is implemented (e.g., one-time password/PIN to a mobile phone, code-generating device supplied by the bank, confirmation email).
*   Data is encrypted – to ensure data is secure during transmission.
*   A firewall is used to protect the bank’s server.
*   Bank servers are kept updated with the latest software / security patches.
*   Bank employees are prohibited from accessing customer data on personal devices / devices outside the company network – to ensure that all devices are protected by the bank’s security measures.

**Privacy:**

*   Customers are informed to specify how the data may be used – if/how it may be shared with third parties.
*   Only authorized personnel will have access to the bank database – not all employees will be able to view data.

**Anonymity:**

*   Ensure the customer’s anonymity is maintained – when data is shared with third parties, the data that could give the identity of a customer must be detached.
*   Reports are kept anonymous – reports cannot allow individuals to be identified.

**_N.B.:_** _T__he response requires an explanation of a policy and not a discussion of the problems themselves. There must be a policy for **each** kind of concern: security, privacy and anonymity and reason(s)._

_Award **\[1\]** for identifying a policy that CBR Bank could use to address the security, privacy and anonymity concerns of its customers and **\[1\]** for a development of the policy identified up to a maximum of **\[2\]**._

_Mark as **\[2\] + \[2\] + \[2\]**._

parts.content:
The chief executive officer (CEO) of _CBR Bank_, Alice McEwan, said in a recent interview, “_CBR Bank_ will be replacing all passwords, PINs and personal verification questions for our online banking and mobile banking with voice biometrics recognition.”

To what extent are the changes proposed by Alice beneficial for both _CBR Bank_’s customers **and** _CBR Bank_’s IT support?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**For customers:**

**Advantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   Customers don’t have to remember a password or PIN code / may not need additional verification such as one-time codes (automation).
*   It is more secure, as voice characteristics are unique.
*   It is harder for others to hack online banking with voice biometrics recognition (security).
*   Some customers may have physical conditions that make entering PINs/passwords difficult – voice recognition will avoid having to type (access/inclusion).

**Disadvantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   The voice recognition system may not accept foreign accents or a range of voices (systems).
*   Illness (such as a cold) can change a person’s voice, making identification difficult (systems).
*   A person’s voice can be easily recorded and used for unauthorized access (security).
*   Someone with very similar voices (e.g., a member of the same family) may be able to gain access to the bank account (security).

**For IT support:**

**Advantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   It is more secure, less likely to be hacked – fewer problems for IT staff to deal with (automation, systems).
*   It is easy for customers to record by themselves – no IT staff required to set up (automation, systems).
*   IT staff do not have to deal with lost password/PIN (automation, systems).

**Disadvantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   The voice recognition system may not accept particular accents – customers cannot access their online banking and will need support (systems).
*   A person’s voice can be easily recorded and used for unauthorized access – customers may complain about unauthorized access, IT staff will have to investigate hacked accounts (security, values).
*   Illness (such as a cold) can change a person’s voice, making identification difficult – a greater number of customers might need to call support to access their own account (systems).
*   When the new system is implemented, _CBR_ _Bank_’s IT support could be overwhelmed with overlooked bugs (reliability).
*   Audio files / biometric templates will require more storage space than passwords / PINs – this may make backing up data more time consuming / require IT support to increase available storage space, etc (data).
*   Initial implementation of the new system may require additional IT support staff.
*   IT support staff may face an increased workload (e.g., if the old system initially has to run parallel to the new system).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** security, authentication, stakeholder, reliability, data, security, change, identity, power, systems, values, ethics, accountability, transparency, access, inclusion

  
_Refer to**SL/ HL paper 1, part c markbands**when awarding marks. These can be found under the 'Your tests' tab > supplemental materials > Digital society markbands and guidance document._

parts.content: Identify two ethical dilemmas associated with AI and describe their implications.
parts.markscheme:
### Ethical Dilemma 1: Bias and Discrimination

AI systems can perpetuate and amplify societal biases present in the training data, leading to unfair and discriminatory outcomes. This can result in:
- Biased decision-making in areas like hiring, lending, and criminal justice
- Reinforcing existing prejudices and stereotypes
- Disproportionately disadvantaging certain groups

### Ethical Dilemma 2: Privacy and Surveillance

The massive data collection and processing capabilities of AI raise privacy concerns:
- Potential for unauthorized access, misuse, or exploitation of personal data
- Erosion of individual privacy through pervasive surveillance and monitoring
- Lack of transparency and control over how personal data is used

### Ethical Dilemma 3: Accountability and Transparency

AI systems can be opaque "black boxes," making it difficult to understand their decision-making processes:
- Lack of accountability for errors or harmful outcomes
- Difficulty in assigning responsibility and liability
- Challenges in ensuring fairness, explainability, and due process

### Ethical Dilemma 4: Automation and Job Displacement

The increasing automation enabled by AI could lead to significant job losses and economic disruption:
- Potential for mass unemployment and income inequality
- Challenges in retraining and reskilling the workforce
- Societal and economic impacts on individuals and communities

<marks>2 marks for identifying and describing any two ethical dilemmas associated with AI</marks>

parts.content: Outline one example of bias in AI-driven hiring processes.

parts.content: Evaluate the ethical challenges of automation in AI, particularly regarding job displacement and economic impacts.
parts.markscheme:
### Key Points
- Automation and AI can lead to job displacement and economic disruption <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - As AI systems become more capable, they may replace human workers in various industries
  - This could result in job losses and economic hardship for those displaced
- Potential negative economic impacts <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Wealth inequality may increase as profits concentrate with companies deploying AI
  - Workers may struggle to find new employment or retrain for different roles
  - Overall economic productivity could decline during the transition period

### Ethical Considerations
- Balancing technological progress with protecting workers' livelihoods <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - AI automation offers potential efficiency gains but at what human cost?
  - Ethical obligation to mitigate negative impacts and support displaced workers

- Ensuring a fair transition and equitable distribution of benefits <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Policies and support systems needed for retraining and job placement
  - Mechanisms to share economic gains from AI across society
  - Potential for universal basic income or other social safety nets

<note>Responses should address both the economic impacts of automation as well as the key ethical tradeoffs and considerations involved. Well-rounded answers weighing different perspectives are expected.</note>

questions.specification:
**Source A** 
 ![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/44405822-6c4a-48ce-b9a1-ac8d731d4eae)
 
 **Source B (from HoloHeritage website)** HoloHeritage is an augmented reality (AR) mobile app used in the Old Port District to provide “walking stories” about local heritage. Visitors scan plaques placed near buildings and waterfront sites, then view an AR layer showing archival photos, narrated stories, and subtitles in three languages. The Cultural Office says recordings were licensed from community storytellers and that a council-appointed panel can mark certain locations as “quiet zones” where recording and user uploads are disabled. The app also allows users to create short remixes by combining approved clips with their own captions, as long as they keep the attribution tag. HoloHeritage collects analytics such as which sites are visited, which languages are selected, and how often stories are shared, and it uses these data to prioritize “popular routes” on the home screen. 
 
 **Source C (same as above)**
 
 9 months after launch, HoloHeritage had 84 000 downloads; 58% of active users were tourists.

Language selection among tourists: 61% dominant-language subtitles, 29% second language, 10% local/heritage language.

22% of users created a remix clip, but only 6% posted it publicly.

Moderation outcomes for user uploads: 71% approved, 19% returned for edits (missing credit/incorrect claim), 10% rejected (restricted content or disrespectful framing).

A visitor survey found 74% agreed the app “made the area feel more meaningful,” while 38% of local respondents agreed the app “encourages performance of culture for outsiders.” 

**Source D (criticism by conservative politician in newspaper)** 

 HoloHeritage is sold as “preservation,” but it also turns culture into a selectable interface: tap a pin, play a story, share a clip. That format subtly rewards the most “postable” heritage (short, dramatic, and easily translated) while sidelining slow knowledge and everyday practices that don’t fit a highlight reel. The Cultural Office points to licensing and quiet zones, yet the power still shifts toward whoever controls the app’s routes, rankings, and data. When popularity determines what appears on the home screen, cultural meaning becomes a metric. There is also a risk of “permission drift”: tourists may assume that if an AR layer exists, it is automatically appropriate to remix, imitate, or monetize. If the community wants digital heritage, it needs more than moderation. It needs governance over data, ranking, and what must remain un-digitized.

parts.content: Define the term augmented reality (AR) as used in Source B.
parts.markscheme:
**Define (Total <marks>2 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Augmented reality involves overlaying digital content onto a user's view of the real world through a device. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This overlay happens in real time and is contextually anchored to specific locations or objects. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award 1 mark for correctly identifying augmented reality as a digital overlay on the real-world view.

Award 1 mark for correctly identifying the real-time or contextual nature of the technology.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses that mention only virtual reality or mixed reality without reference to overlaying digital content onto the real world should receive 0 marks.</note>


**Sample answer**


Augmented reality is a technology that overlays digital content onto a user's view of the real world through a device such as a phone or tablet.

This overlay happens in real time and is often anchored to specific places or objects, meaning the digital content responds to the user's location or what they are looking at.

parts.content: Suggest two ways the designers of HoloHeritage could increase meaningful engagement with the heritage language, using evidence from Source C.
parts.markscheme:
**Suggest (Total <marks>4 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Make the heritage language the default subtitle option for some stories or add a prompt encouraging users to try the heritage language option. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This targets the low heritage language usage shown in Source C, where only 10 percent of tourists select the local or heritage language option. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Add bilingual modes showing both heritage and dominant language subtitles to reduce barriers while increasing exposure to the heritage language. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This addresses the language selection distribution in Source C, where most tourists choose dominant language subtitles. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Create incentives for heritage language engagement such as badges or route unlocks tied to learning tasks rather than social media sharing. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This responds to the low heritage language selection and the finding that only 6 percent of users post remixes publicly. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Develop guided heritage language routes featured on the home screen using the app's popularity routing mechanism. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This leverages the app's existing analytics to promote heritage language content based on usage patterns in Source C. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Add community-created pronunciation or audio support in the heritage language to increase meaningful engagement beyond subtitles. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This provides deeper linguistic interaction addressing the limited engagement with heritage language content. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to 2 marks per design suggestion.

Award 1 mark for a plausible design change.

Award 1 mark for explicitly linking the suggestion to evidence from Source C.

Maximum 4 marks for two complete suggestions.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses must provide two distinct design changes. Suggestions not linked to evidence from Source C can receive a maximum of 2 marks. Single suggestions cannot earn more than 2 marks.</note>


**Sample answer**


The designers could make the heritage language the default subtitle option for certain stories or add a gentle prompt encouraging users to try the heritage language. This directly targets the low usage shown in Source C where only 10 percent of tourists select the heritage language option.

Alternatively, the app could add bilingual modes showing both heritage and dominant language subtitles at the same time. This would reduce barriers for tourists while increasing their exposure to the heritage language, addressing the language selection distribution where most users choose dominant language subtitles.

parts.content: Compare what Source C and Source D indicate about how HoloHeritage may change cultural participation for locals and tourists.
parts.markscheme:
**Compare (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Source C indicates that tourists constitute 58 percent of active users and mostly choose dominant language subtitles at 61 percent. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source D argues that the app format rewards postable heritage and translation-friendly content, potentially shaping tourist-facing participation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C shows that 74 percent of visitors agreed the app made the area feel more meaningful. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C also reveals that 38 percent of local respondents agreed the app encourages performance of culture for outsiders. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source D claims that cultural meaning becomes a metric through rankings and popularity routes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source D suggests this shifts participation toward what performs well rather than what is culturally meaningful. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C shows moderation outcomes with approvals, returns, and rejections, implying some control over user uploads. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source D argues that moderation is insufficient without governance over ranking, data, and decisions about what remains undigitized. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Both sources acknowledge some level of control but differ on the adequacy and direction of that control. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award 2 marks per effective comparison using both sources, up to 6 marks.

Award 1 mark for identifying a relevant point from one source.

Award 1 mark for comparing it with a relevant point from the other source.

Responses using only one source can receive a maximum of 3 marks.

Non-comparative responses can receive a maximum of 4 marks.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Students must explicitly connect ideas between Source C and Source D to earn full marks. Listing points from each source without comparison will be limited to 4 marks. Points must be substantive and directly relevant to cultural participation.</note>


**Sample answer**


Source C and Source D present different pictures of cultural participation for locals and tourists. Source C reports that tourists make up 58 percent of active users and predominantly select dominant language subtitles, while Source D argues that the app format rewards short, postable, and easily translated heritage, shaping tourist participation toward what is easily shareable rather than culturally detailed.

Source C also shows that most visitors found the area more meaningful when using the app, but a significant proportion of locals felt it encouraged performance of culture for outsiders. Source D extends this concern by arguing that popularity rankings turn cultural meaning into a metric. While Source C suggests some control through moderation outcomes, Source D argues that this control is insufficient without governance over ranking systems, data, and decisions about what should remain undigitized.

parts.content: To what extent does HoloHeritage strengthen cultural heritage rather than commodify it? With reference to all the sources (A–D) and your own knowledge of the Digital Society course, consider ownership and control, representation, platform metrics (ranking/visibility), and the ethics of digitizing and remixing cultural material.
parts.markscheme:
**To what extent (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* HoloHeritage strengthens cultural heritage by increasing access and engagement, evidenced by 84,000 downloads and 74 percent of visitors feeling the area was more meaningful. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The app supports strengthening through licensed recordings and attribution rules that ensure ethical use and credit for community storytellers. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Protective measures such as quiet zones and upload moderation show some consideration for preserving cultural integrity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* These protective measures help control how cultural content is shared and who can contribute. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* HoloHeritage risks commodifying culture by using popularity-based data to determine home screen routes, pushing cultural visibility toward engagement metrics. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source D argues that the selectable interface format rewards short, dramatic, and easily translated content over slow knowledge and everyday practices. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This creates pressure to present heritage in ways that generate shares and likes rather than preserving authentic cultural expression. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The remixing feature raises ethical concerns about permission drift where tourists may assume they can monetise or inappropriately use cultural content simply because it is digitised. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The finding that 38 percent of locals feel the app encourages performance for outsiders supports the commodification argument. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Course knowledge includes platform power in controlling ranking and visibility systems. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Cultural appropriation concerns arise when communities lose control over how their heritage is presented and monetised digitally. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Data governance issues emerge through the collection and use of visitor analytics without clear community consent or oversight. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Procedural safeguards such as community oversight boards, transparent ranking criteria, and limits on digitisation could help balance preservation and accessibility. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Differential access controls could protect sensitive cultural knowledge while still allowing appropriate public engagement. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to 12 marks for a reasoned position that synthesises all sources with relevant own knowledge and reaches a justified conclusion.

Award up to 6 marks for use of source material alone.

Award up to 6 marks for use of own knowledge alone.

Responses using only sources or only own knowledge can receive a maximum of 8 marks.


**Marking guidance**


<note>To reach the highest mark bands, students must present a balanced evaluation that considers both strengthening and commodifying arguments. Responses should explicitly reference ownership and control, representation, platform metrics, and ethics of digitisation. A clear conclusion about the extent of strengthening versus commodifying is required for full marks.</note>


**Sample answer**


HoloHeritage both strengthens and commodifies cultural heritage, though the commodifying effects may ultimately outweigh the strengthening benefits. The app strengthens heritage by increasing access and engagement, with 84,000 downloads and 74 percent of visitors reporting that the area felt more meaningful. Licensed recordings and attribution rules show an attempt to ensure ethical use and proper credit for community storytellers, while quiet zones and moderation demonstrate some commitment to protecting cultural integrity.

However, these strengthening measures are undermined by significant commodifying risks. The use of popularity data to determine home screen routes turns cultural visibility into a metric. As Source D argues, the interface rewards short, dramatic, and easily translated content, sidelining slow knowledge and everyday practices. The remixing feature raises ethical concerns about permission drift, and the finding that 38 percent of locals feel the app encourages performance for outsiders supports the view that culture is being shaped for tourist consumption.

From a Digital Society perspective, this illustrates platform power in ranking and visibility systems and raises concerns about cultural appropriation and data governance. Safeguards such as community oversight boards, transparent ranking criteria, and limits on digitisation could help rebalance preservation and access. Overall, while HoloHeritage improves access to heritage, it commodifies cultural meaning to a significant extent through platform logic and metrics.

parts.content: Explain two limitations of copyright laws
parts.markscheme:
Limitations of copyright laws:

- Copyright laws only protect the expression of ideas, not the ideas themselves. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Anyone can use the underlying ideas, concepts, or principles expressed in a copyrighted work.

- Copyright protection is limited in duration. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Copyright eventually expires, and works enter the public domain, allowing free use.
  - Example: In most countries, copyright lasts for the life of the author plus 50-70 years.

- Fair use/fair dealing exceptions allow limited use of copyrighted works without permission. <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Examples: Criticism, commentary, news reporting, research, teaching, etc.
  - The amount used must be reasonable and not harm the market for the work.

- Copyright laws have limitations on enforcement across borders. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Copyright is territorial, and laws vary between countries.
  - Enforcing copyright internationally can be challenging.

<note>Award 1 mark for each well-explained limitation, up to a maximum of 6 marks.</note>

parts.content: The evolution of the internet and computing has made it easier for people to illegally copy and distribute works of art. Discuss the impact this is having on the music industry.
parts.markscheme:
Some points could include: 1. Loss of Revenue: Illegal downloading and sharing of music files lead to a reduction in revenue for artists, record labels, and other stakeholders.
With easier access to pirated music, consumers may choose not to pay for songs or albums, directly affecting sales and reducing income for the music industry. 2. Challenges for Emerging Artists: New artists face difficulties in establishing themselves due to the loss of potential revenue streams.
They rely on album sales and streaming royalties to fund their careers, and piracy undermines these opportunities, making it harder to sustain a livelihood solely through music. 3. Shift in Business Models: The industry has had to adapt, with a shift toward streaming services (e.g., Spotify, Apple Music) as a way to counteract piracy.
While these services help reduce illegal downloads, they often provide lower per-stream revenue for artists compared to traditional album sales. 4. Impact on Creativity and Production Quality: Lower revenue may mean fewer resources for high-quality production, as smaller labels and independent artists have less to invest in their music.
This can limit creative exploration and reduce the overall quality and variety of music available to consumers. 5. Increased Reliance on Live Performances and Merchandise: To counterbalance losses from album sales, artists increasingly rely on live performances, tours, and merchandise as primary revenue sources.
However, live performances are not feasible for all artists, especially for those with physical or logistical limitations, meaning they are more severely impacted by illegal distribution. 6. Legal and Ethical Concerns: Piracy raises ethical issues, as it undermines artists' rights to their own work.
The music industry has implemented stricter copyright laws and taken legal action against file-sharing platforms, but enforcement remains challenging due to the global nature of the internet. 7. Potential Positive Exposure: On a positive note, free access to music can increase exposure for some artists, allowing them to reach a wider audience and potentially gain fans who may later pay for concert tickets or official merchandise.
However, this benefit is uncertain and varies widely depending on the artist's popularity and audience. Conclusion: The illegal copying and distribution of music have forced the industry to adapt, with mixed results. While streaming and alternative revenue sources provide some compensation, piracy continues to pose financial and ethical challenges, particularly for smaller artists. The industry must balance between protecting intellectual property and embracing new digital business models that meet changing consumer behaviors. 8 marks Valid points should be accepted with 1 mark for the point and an additional point for a supporting explanation/example.

parts.content: Discuss the implications of AI-driven automation on employment, focusing on the balance between economic efficiency and potential job displacement.
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of AI-driven automation and its general effect on employment.
- Limited discussion on economic efficiency or job displacement, with little depth or balance between the two.
- Few examples or specifics to illustrate key points.


"AI-driven automation can lead to job loss in some industries."
"Automation can make work faster and more efficient."
</commonMistake>

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear explanation of AI-driven automation's dual impact on employment, including both potential economic efficiency and job displacement.
- Some discussion of benefits (e.g., improved productivity and cost reduction) alongside basic mention of risks (e.g., job loss in specific sectors).
- Examples provided to support points, though may lack depth or variety.


"AI-driven automation increases economic efficiency by speeding up processes and reducing costs, which benefits businesses."
"However, certain jobs, especially in manufacturing and repetitive tasks, may be at risk of displacement."
Examples might include references to automation in retail (self-checkout systems) or manufacturing (robotic assembly lines).
</commonMistake>

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- In-depth evaluation of the implications of AI-driven automation on employment, considering both economic efficiency and job displacement.
- Balanced analysis of benefits and risks, showing nuanced understanding of how automation can drive economic growth but also result in challenges for workforce adaptation.
- Detailed examination of sectors where automation is most impactful (e.g., manufacturing, retail, transportation) and where new opportunities may emerge (e.g., tech support, AI maintenance).
- Examples used effectively to illustrate points, with insights into both the positive impacts (e.g., efficiency, growth in new sectors) and negative impacts (e.g., job displacement, need for reskilling).

$$
\text{Economic Efficiency} \leftrightarrow \text{Job Displacement}
$$


"AI-driven automation enhances economic efficiency by reducing labor costs and increasing productivity, allowing companies to produce goods and services at lower prices, potentially increasing consumer access."
"However, automation can displace jobs in sectors where repetitive tasks are common. For instance, autonomous vehicles could reduce the demand for truck drivers, and robotic process automation could decrease the need for administrative roles."
"There is also a shift in job creation, with new roles emerging in AI development, maintenance, and oversight, but this requires significant retraining and may not offset losses in low-skill jobs."
</commonMistake>

<examTip>Balancing these effects is difficult, as job displacement impacts socio-economic stability. Policymakers might need to consider interventions such as retraining programs, universal basic income, or support for sectors heavily affected by automation.</examTip>

parts.content: To what extent does bandwidth availability impact the quality of online activities like streaming and gaming, considering both user experience and network limitations?
parts.markscheme:
- Bandwidth is the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection in a given time period <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher bandwidth allows for faster data transfer rates and better quality for online activities like:
    - Video streaming (higher resolution, less buffering) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Online gaming (lower latency, smoother gameplay) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Video conferencing (higher quality audio/video) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- User experience is impacted by bandwidth limitations:
    - Low bandwidth leads to poor quality video/audio, lag, buffering <marks>1 mark</marks>
    <commonMistake>Confusing bandwidth with internet speed</commonMistake>

- Network limitations that impact bandwidth availability:
    - Type of internet connection (e.g. fiber optic vs DSL) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Distance from internet service provider <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Network congestion during peak usage times <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Shared bandwidth among multiple users/devices <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Solutions to increase bandwidth:
    - Upgrade to a higher-tier internet plan <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Use wired Ethernet instead of WiFi <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Optimize home network (e.g. better router placement) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Answers should consider both the user perspective and technical network factors.</note>

questions.specification:
### AI and Transparency in Decision-Making
AI is used in critical areas such as finance, healthcare, and criminal justice, where decision-making transparency is crucial. ""Black box"" algorithms, however, lack transparency, making it difficult for users to understand or challenge AI-driven decisions, which can affect trust and fairness.



![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ec4096a8-1afd-4152-9af1-bae97f68f6a8)

parts.content: To what extent should transparency be a priority in AI-driven decision-making, particularly in high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice?
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of transparency in AI.
- Limited discussion on why transparency might be important, with minimal reference to high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice.
- Few or no examples to support points.

Example Points:

- "Transparency is needed to see how AI decisions are made."
- "It is important in high-stakes areas like finance."

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear explanation of the concept of transparency and its importance in AI decision-making.
- Some discussion of high-stakes areas (finance, criminal justice), mentioning the potential consequences of opaque AI systems.
- Basic mention of risks in not prioritizing transparency, such as accountability and fairness issues.
- Some examples to illustrate key points, though may lack depth.

Example Points:

- "Transparency in AI is crucial for accountability, especially in areas like criminal justice where lives can be directly affected."
- "In finance, a lack of transparency could lead to unfair credit decisions, impacting people's financial stability."
- Examples may include basic references to credit scoring or criminal sentencing decisions influenced by AI without deep analysis.

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- In-depth evaluation of the necessity of transparency in AI, particularly in high-stakes fields.
- Comprehensive discussion of ethical and practical implications, including how transparency supports accountability, trust, and fairness.
- Detailed analysis of risks associated with opaque AI systems, such as potential bias, wrongful decisions, and the challenges to correcting errors without transparency.
- Balanced argument with consideration of challenges to implementing transparency, like trade-offs with proprietary information or the complexity of AI models.
- Use of specific examples to illustrate implications in both finance (e.g., credit scoring or loan approvals) and criminal justice (e.g., sentencing algorithms or predictive policing).

Example Points:

- "Transparency in AI decision-making is essential in high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice, where decisions have far-reaching impacts on individuals' lives. It ensures that affected parties can understand, contest, and seek recourse for decisions that impact them."
- "In criminal justice, lack of transparency in AI-driven sentencing algorithms can lead to biased outcomes without accountability, eroding public trust. Transparency is needed to scrutinize whether the system perpetuates existing biases, particularly against marginalized groups."
- "In finance, opaque algorithms used in credit scoring can disadvantage certain applicants without providing a clear reason, limiting access to financial opportunities."
- <note>Challenges: Achieving transparency can be difficult when models are complex or proprietary, but efforts like explainable AI and regulatory oversight could help balance transparency with innovation.</note>

parts.content: Discuss the ethical implications of user data collection and surveillance on social networks, focusing on privacy, security, and consent.
parts.markscheme:
### Privacy and consent <marks>4 marks</marks>
- User data collection often happens without explicit consent or transparency
- Privacy concerns over personal data being collected, shared, and used for purposes not intended by users
- Lack of control over how personal data is used and disseminated
- Potential for data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information

### Security risks <marks>3 marks</marks>
- Collection of user data creates security vulnerabilities and risks of hacking/data breaches
- Centralized data storage makes platforms an attractive target for cyber attacks
- Stolen data can be used for identity theft, fraud, or other malicious activities
$$
\text{Risk} \propto \text{Amount of data collected}
$$

### Surveillance and social control <marks>3 marks</marks>
- User data enables mass surveillance and monitoring of online activities
- Potential for data to be used for social control, manipulation, or discrimination
- Chilling effects on free speech and expression due to perceived monitoring
- Asymmetry of power between platforms with user data and individual users

### Ethical considerations <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Lack of transparency and informed consent violates ethical principles
- User autonomy and right to privacy are undermined
- Utilitarian arguments about benefits must be weighed against ethical costs
- Need for stronger data protection laws and user-centric data governance models

questions.specification:
**Social media and political tension**

National governments sometimes block websites at a time of political tension. One example was in 2011, when a government prevented its citizens’ access to _Twitter_, _Facebook_ and _YouTube_. Images on these websites were considered inappropriate, with the potential to cause hatred, violence and political unrest in that country. The national government gave direct orders to the internet service providers (ISPs) to block access to these sites for four hours. The aim was to give enough time for site officials to remove the offending images from their websites.

Despite these attempts by the government, some citizens were still able to access these images using proxy servers.

Many people, such as academics, believe that this approach is not appropriate and that governments should focus on the education of their citizens so that they are able to make informed decisions about how to react to potentially offensive information on websites and social media.

parts.content:
Many schools block access to social networking websites like _Twitter_, _Facebook_ and _YouTube_. However, other schools are investigating two different options:

*   Monitoring the network to view what websites the students are viewing.
*   Giving different ages of students different levels of access to social media sites.

Analyse these **two** options.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Monitoring the network:**

*   It prevents cyberbullying – keep students safe (values).
*   It teaches students responsibility – digital citizenship (values).
*   It protects students from sharing inappropriate information and images about themselves (values).
*   It identifies any objectionable/inappropriate content that students can access on these sites.
*   It gathers data/information for future policies for the use (or not) of social media.
*   Students of all ages would have access to the same websites, regardless of age – younger students could have access to objectionable/inappropriate content for their age.
*   Privacy of students – school would have access to see what the students are viewing.

**Giving different ages different levels of access:**

*   Some websites have age restrictions – should only be accessed if age appropriate (values).
*   Younger students should not be exposed to objectionable/inappropriate content that is acceptable for older students.
*   Schools have a responsibility to ensure the use of the network is appropriate to various ages.
*   Younger students may not be able to differentiate between true intentions of online friendships (expression).
*   Older students need to learn responsible digital citizenship and take responsibility for their actions (values, trust).
*   Awareness of the benefits and drawbacks of social media sites is gained as the student matures (values).
*   Blocking sites by age could cause issues – blocking content that is needed by older students granting different levels of access may be more cost-effective than implementing an ongoing monitoring system (power, values).

  
**Keywords:** home, online, social, relationships, responsibility, trust, software applications, apps, objectionable content, change, expression, identity, power, values, ethics

parts.content:
Many citizens have raised concerns about the surveillance of their web browsing history or the censorship of selected websites by their national government.

To what extent is it appropriate for national governments to use surveillance and censorship to control citizens’ access to websites?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Reasons that surveillance and censorship to control citizens’ access to websites is acceptable:**

*   It can keep children from being victims of cyberbullying, sex trafficking and pornography, objectionable/inappropriate content.
*   It can control/monitor various illegal activities (values).
*   It helps strengthen national security with laws against hacking and impose large fines and punishments (values).
*   It can lessen the incidence of identity theft.
*   Governments may be best informed to determine what may, or may not, be appropriate for their citizens (values).

**Reasons that surveillance and censorship to control citizens’ access to websites is not acceptable:**

*   It removes citizens’ freedom of expression.
*   It can be used to keep information from citizens that may infringe on their privacy and/or anonymity (trust).
*   It can be expensive for the government or may require too many resources for the benefits it brings (systems).
*   It may accidentally block sites that should not be blocked (systems).
*   Knowledge is power.
*   It can be used to identify people who oppose the government (values, expression).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** Automation, connectivity, change, power, systems, values, ethics, machine learning, accountability, transparency, autonomy, surveillance

questions.specification:
### Source A: Types of Digital Media and Their Evolution
Digital media encompasses a wide range of formats, including text, images, video, and gaming. User-generated content such as blogs and live streams allows individuals to create and share information. Recently, AI-generated media and deepfakes have emerged, raising both excitement and ethical concerns.

For example, YouTube enables users to upload and share video content globally, while deepfake technology has sparked discussions on authenticity and ethical use.


### Source B: Characteristics of Digital Media
Digital media stands out for its rapid sharing, interactivity, and efficient storage capabilities. Unlike traditional media, digital media allows for non-linear consumption, and its content can be accessed across multiple devices due to media convergence.

For instance, social media platforms combine text, images, and video, allowing users to interact and share diverse content in real-time, often across various formats and devices.



### Source C: Immersive Digital Media Technologies
Immersive digital media, such as Augmented Reality (AR), Virtual Reality (VR), and Mixed Reality (MR), creates engaging experiences. These technologies are applied in gaming, education, and training, bridging physical and digital worlds.

For example, VR games provide a fully immersive environment, while AR enhances real-world experiences by overlaying digital information, such as navigation aids, on physical surroundings.


### Source D: Digital Media and Psychological Impact
Digital media's accessibility raises psychological concerns, including addiction, anxiety, and depression. Studies indicate that excessive social media use can lead to increased mental health risks, especially among younger users.

For example, platforms like Instagram create feedback loops where users seek validation through likes, which can impact mental well-being and increase screen time.

<question>
With reference to Source D, outline one psychological concern associated with digital media use and explain its impact on users.
</question>

<marks>2 marks</marks>

Outline of psychological concern:

- Anxiety/depression/low self-esteem
- Cyberbullying/online harassment
- Fear of missing out (FOMO)
- Distraction/lack of focus
- Social isolation/lack of face-to-face interaction

<marks>2 marks</marks>

Explanation of impact:
- Addiction/compulsive use: Constant checking of devices/apps, neglecting other activities/responsibilities, withdrawal symptoms when unable to access digital media

- Distraction/lack of focus: Difficulty concentrating on tasks, reduced productivity, poor academic/work performance
- Social isolation/lack of face-to-face interaction: Reduced social skills, difficulty forming meaningful relationships, loneliness

parts.content: Define the term ‘uncanny valley’.
parts.markscheme:
The term 'uncanny valley' refers to the phenomenon where a computer-animated figure or humanoid robot bearing a near-identical resemblance to a human being arouses a sense of unease or revulsion in the person viewing it. <marks>1 mark</marks>

The 'valley' refers to the dip in a graph of the perception of a robot's likeability, as its appearance approaches, but fails to fully replicate, a human being. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline two reasons why robot developers would want humans to develop an emotional relationship with robots.

- To make robots seem more trustworthy/relatable

parts.content: Explain two reasons why there are underdeveloped laws about the use of robots in some countries.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following reasons explained up to a maximum of <marks>6 marks</marks>:

- Robotics is a relatively new technology and laws often lag behind technological advancements
- There may be disagreements or lack of consensus on how to regulate robotics
- Lawmakers may lack understanding or expertise in robotics technology

- Different countries have different cultural attitudes and priorities towards robotics regulation

Responses that simply list reasons without explaining them should not be awarded any marks.

parts.content: Identify two types of computing networks and describe their primary purposes.
parts.markscheme:
### Types of networks and their purposes

- LAN (Local Area Network) <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Used to connect computers/devices within a limited geographical area such as a home, office, or school
  - Allows sharing of resources (e.g. printers, files) and communication between connected devices

- WAN (Wide Area Network) <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Covers a large geographical area, spanning cities, countries, or continents
  - Connects multiple smaller networks (LANs) together
  - Enables long-distance communication and data transfer over long distances

<note>Award 1 mark for each correctly identified network type and its primary purpose.</note>

### Key Points
- A VPN (Virtual Private Network) creates a secure encrypted connection over the internet <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$
\text{Data} \xrightarrow{\text{Encrypted}} \text{Internet} \xrightarrow{\text{Decrypted}} \text{Private Network}
$$

- VPNs use encryption protocols like IPsec, SSL/TLS to secure data transmission <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Authentication mechanisms ensure only authorized users can access the VPN <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**How to appropriately interact in an online environment**

Increasing numbers of parents are claiming digital technologies that monitor online activities are the best way to protect their children from offensive content. A number of companies, such as _mSpy_ and _Qustodio_, have developed online monitoring apps.

Other parents claim it is not necessary to monitor their children’s online behaviour, preferring to encourage their children to develop the knowledge and skills to make appropriate choices.

Parents can consider two different interventions to ensure their children use the online environment appropriately:

1.  Monitor their children’s online behaviour using apps
2.  Encourage their children to develop the knowledge and skills to use the online environment and make appropriate choices.

With reference to real-life scenarios, evaluate these two interventions.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Intervention 1: Online monitoring (claim)**

**Advantages of monitoring online behaviour using apps:**

*   Safety from exposure to inappropriate/offensive/objectionable content and interactions with people that have not been approved by the parent (online predators, etc).
*   Monitor interactions between the child’s social groups (e.g., online bullying / transparency).
*   Monitor time usage to ensure the child is not spending too much time online.

**Disadvantages of monitoring online behaviour using apps:**

*   Children may not know that their parents monitor their messages and online activities, which can be detrimental to trust and relationships within the family (ethics, values).
*   Trust issues from parental control may lead to the child seeking other online avenues behind their parents’ backs. Where does the power lie?
*   Children have a right to privacy, which these apps refute and deny (ethics, values).
*   Younger children may need to be protected, but teenagers may feel they have a right to exchange messages in confidentiality (transparency) with other people without their parents spying on them.
*   The use of apps could involve data being sent to the app developers. which potentially introduces new privacy issues (transparency).
*   The apps can be costly.
*   Does not prepare the child for later life.

**Intervention 2: Developing online knowledge and skills (counter-claim)**

**Advantages of encouraging the development of online skills (claims supporting the intervention):**

*   Develops lifelong skills/values that stay with the child into adulthood.
*   Promotes trust between parent and child (ethics, values).

**Disadvantages of encouraging the development of knowledge and online skills:**

*   May be inappropriate for younger children, who are not mature enough to make appropriate choices in the complex online environment (acceptability, ethics, values).
*   Developing skills/\[ethical\]values is a learning curve and mistakes may still be made, the consequences of which may be the safety of the child (i.e., online predators/cyberbullying), security (i.e., downloading a virus or malware), or cost (i.e., in-app purchases).
*   The online environment is constantly changing, and parents would have to ensure that they are up to date with good safety practices to ensure they are giving the best advice (ethics, values).

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts.  
  
_

**Keywords:** home, online, health, social, family, relationships, trust, advice, software applications, apps, offensive, inappropriate, objectionable, content, change, expression, transparency, identity, power, ethics, values
